analysis.quote: Downs is a true do-it-all safety who plays with elite instincts and range on the back end. He flies around the field, makes plays in coverage, and isn't afraid to come downhill and hit. He feels like the type of centerpiece defensive back you build around, and dropping him into this secondary would instantly raise the ceiling of the entire unit.

Pregnon is a massive interior presence who brings power and physicality to the offensive line. He moves people in the run game and holds up well in pass protection when he's locked in. This pick is all about adding strength in the trenches, and he has the tools to develop into a long-term starter inside.

Lawrence is an explosive edge rusher with the kind of burst that jumps off the screen. He wins with speed and bend, and when he gets going downhill, he's tough to stop. The production is there, but consistency will be the key at the next level. Still, the upside as a disruptive pass rusher is easy to get excited about.

Louis is a high-energy linebacker who's always around the ball and plays faster than most at the position. He reads quickly, closes hard, and brings an aggressive edge to every snap. His performance at the Senior Bowl only boosted the hype, and he feels like the type of player who could outplay his draft slot in a hurry.

Rivers is a scrappy, competitive corner who plays bigger than his size and isn't afraid to challenge receivers. He's quick, sticky in coverage, and shows good awareness when the ball is in the air. He may not have elite size, but his attitude and play style make him a fun depth piece with upside.

Hurst brings size and vertical ability to the receiver room with the ability to stretch the field. He tracks the ball well downfield and can win in contested situations. There's some development needed in his route tree, but the physical tools and big-play ability are definitely there.

Barham is a versatile front-seven weapon who can line up in multiple spots and bring pressure from different angles. He plays with power and urgency, and there's a real edge to how he attacks blockers. He's the kind of piece defensive coaches love to move around and get creative with.

Stephens is a tough, physical interior lineman who fits the mold of a classic grinder in the trenches. He plays with good leverage, stays active through the whistle, and does the dirty work in the run game. He may not be flashy, but he brings a reliable presence and depth to the interior.

Marshall is a quick, competitive corner who thrives in man coverage and brings solid effort on every snap. He's aggressive at the catch point and isn't afraid to get involved in run support. This is the kind of depth pick that can carve out a role on special teams while developing on defense.

Harvey is an athletic corner with good movement skills and the ability to stay attached in coverage. He flashes playmaking ability when the ball comes his way and has the tools to develop into a solid depth option. At this stage, it's all about traits and upside, and Harvey checks those boxes.

Green is a big-armed dual-threat QB with explosive athleticism and intriguing upside. He threw for over 3,000 yards and 28 touchdowns last season while rushing for 600+ yards, showing he can make plays with both his arm and legs. This is a developmental pick for the Dolphins, someone who could push Malik Willis in practice and maybe surprise down the line.
